matter.title: Small Claims of Grace C. Roe v. Maria Q. Fairbanks
matter.summary: Grace C. Roe, the sole proprietor of a freelance web-design practice, brings a small claims action in Sagadahoc County District Court against a former customer, Maria Q. Fairbanks, to collect the unpaid balance arising from unpaid invoice for completed services; the claim is within the $6,000 Maine small claims limit.

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe, the sole proprietor of a freelance web-design practice, brings a small claims action in Sagadahoc County District Court against a former customer, Maria Q. Fairbanks, to collect the unpaid balance arising from unpaid invoice for completed services; the claim is within the $6,000 Maine small claims limit.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe owns and operates a freelance web-design practice as a sole proprietor in Sagadahoc County, Maine. In early 2025 the business agreed to perform work for Maria Q. Fairbanks, a customer, and completed that work on 2025-05-27. The matter arises from unpaid invoice for completed services.

The business billed Maria an invoice amount of $3427 for the work. Maria paid only $573 toward that bill and then stopped responding, leaving the unpaid balance (the invoice amount less the amount paid) outstanding. Grace sent a written demand for payment on 2025-12-28, but Maria neither paid the balance nor disputed the underlying charges in writing.

Grace now seeks a small claims judgment for the unpaid balance, together with court costs and post-judgment interest. Because the total amount in controversy is at or below $6,000, the claim falls within the jurisdiction of the Maine District Court small claims docket under 14 M.R.S. § 7482, and Grace intends to file in Bath.
undisputed_facts: Grace C. Roe operates a freelance web-design practice as a sole proprietor.
The work was completed on 2025-05-27 and Maria Q. Fairbanks was billed $3427.
Maria Q. Fairbanks paid $573 and a balance remains unpaid.
Grace sent a written demand for payment on 2025-12-28.
disputed_facts: Whether the full invoiced amount was actually owed, or whether Maria claims the work was incomplete or defective.
Whether the parties ever agreed on the price and scope of the work, given that the engagement was based on a written work order the customer signed.
Whether Grace is entitled to the claimed pre-judgment interest in addition to the unpaid balance.

timeline.event: Grace completed the work for Maria and issued the invoice for $3427.
Statement of Claim (SC-001) prepared for filing in Bath District Court.
Grace sent a written demand letter for the unpaid balance.

third_parties.name: Lighthouse Outfitters Corp.
third_parties.relevance: The plaintiff consulted this agency about recovering the unpaid balance before deciding to file in small claims court.

third_parties.name: Marcus H. Moe
third_parties.relevance: Performed the work for the defendant and can testify that the services were completed as agreed.

third_parties.name: Downeast Federal Savings
third_parties.role: Bank where the check was drawn
third_parties.relevance: The bank on which the defendant's returned check was drawn; can confirm the check was dishonored for insufficient funds.

exchanges.question: How much does the customer owe you, and how did the debt come about?
exchanges.answer: I run a freelance web-design practice. I billed Maria $3427 for the work, they only paid $573, and the rest of the balance is still unpaid. This all comes out of unpaid invoice for completed services.

exchanges.question: What did the two of you agree to, and is any of it in writing?
exchanges.answer: We agreed on the work and the price up front. The job was based on a written work order the customer signed, and either way I have the invoice and our messages back and forth, so the terms are clear.

exchanges.question: What have you done so far to try to collect?
exchanges.answer: I called and emailed several times, then I sent a written demand letter on 2025-12-28. I even talked to a collections agency, but Maria just went quiet and never paid the balance.

exchanges.question: Are you looking for anything beyond the unpaid balance itself?
exchanges.answer: I want the balance, my filing fee back, and post-judgment interest. Whether I can add the pre-judgment interest I figured is up to you, but I do not want to be out of pocket for taking them to court.

exchanges.question: What does a good resolution look like for you?
exchanges.answer: Honestly, I just want to be paid what I am actually owed. I am not going to keep spending money chasing this forever, but I am not willing to simply write off the whole debt either.

goals: Obtain a small claims judgment for the unpaid balance owed by Maria Q. Fairbanks.
Recover court costs, including the filing fee, from the defendant.
Resolve the matter efficiently without spending more than the debt is worth.
priorities: Being made whole on the unpaid balance.
Keeping collection costs proportionate to the amount in dispute.
A clean, enforceable judgment that supports later collection if needed.
desired_outcomes: A money judgment for the unpaid balance plus allowable court costs and post-judgment interest.
Prompt payment, or a realistic payment plan, from Maria.
non_starters: Writing off the full debt and walking away with nothing.

constraints.budget: Modest; prefers the streamlined small claims process over a full civil suit.
constraints.timeline: Wants to file and obtain a judgment within the current court term.

issues.issue: Account stated for the unpaid invoice
sub_questions: Was an invoice rendered and retained without timely objection?
Does the defendant's partial payment acknowledge the debt?
issues.client_position: The invoice was rendered, partially paid, and never disputed, establishing an account stated.

issues.issue: Breach of the agreement to pay for the work
sub_questions: Did the parties form an enforceable agreement on price and scope?
Did the plaintiff perform and the defendant fail to pay?
issues.client_position: The plaintiff performed the agreed work and the defendant breached by failing to pay the balance.

issues.issue: Statute of limitations for a contract claim
sub_questions: Did the claim accrue within the six-year limitations period?
Does the recent completion date keep the action timely?
issues.client_position: The debt arose in 2025, well within the six-year limitations period, so the action is timely.

expert_opinions.topic: Authentication of the account ledger and computation of the balance due
expert_opinions.opinion: The ledger accurately reflects the invoiced charges and partial payment, leaving the claimed unpaid balance.
expert_opinions.basis: Review of the invoice, the account ledger, and the record of payments received.

evidence.title: Account / payment ledger
evidence.description: The business ledger showing the charge, the $573 partial payment, and the outstanding balance.

evidence.title: Returned / bounced check
evidence.description: A check from the defendant that was returned by the bank for insufficient funds.

evidence.title: Demand letter
evidence.description: The written demand for payment sent to the defendant on 2025-12-28.

evidence.title: Email and text exchanges acknowledging the debt
evidence.description: Messages in which the defendant discusses the work and acknowledges owing for it.

evidence.title: Unpaid invoice
evidence.description: The invoice billed to the defendant for $3427 that remains unpaid.

amounts.label: Invoice amount billed
amounts.basis: Total charged to the defendant for the work.

amounts.label: Amount already paid by defendant
amounts.basis: Partial payment received before the customer stopped paying.

facts.contract_status: based on a written work order the customer signed
